posts.post_caption:
A Proud Moment for Titan PMR – EPS is Now Fully Rolled Out Across Wales

On 17th November 2023, Titan PMR made history.

We became the first PMR to deploy EPS in Wales, with Wellington Road Pharmacy in Rhyl leading the way.

Patients there were the first in Wales to experience a fully digital prescription process.

Now, EPS is fully rolled out across all Titan pharmacies in Wales, marking a major milestone in our mission to transform pharmacy care.

What does this mean for pharmacies?

✅ No more paper prescriptions → Fully digital workflow.
✅ Faster, safer dispensing → Fewer errors, better stock control.
✅ Less admin, more efficiency → Simplified reimbursements & operations.

💬 "Visiting Wellington Road Pharmacy, I witnessed firsthand the benefits of the Electronic Prescription Service, which allows GPs to send prescriptions securely online to the patient's choice of community pharmacy, eliminating the need for paper forms."
— Eluned Morgan MS, Minister for Health and Social Services

💡 This is just the beginning. At Titan PMR, we’re committed to driving the future of pharmacy innovation.

🔗 Visit titanpmr.com to see how we’re shaping what’s next.

#EPSWales #PharmacyEfficiency #DigitalPharmacy #PharmacyInnovation #BetterPatientCare

posts.post_caption:
Pharmacies can boost revenue and patient care.

Here’s how they’re doing it

Pharmacies in the UK are stepping up as GPs face increasing demand. The upside? Every consultation brings extra NHS income for your pharmacy.

But here’s the challenge:
Most pharmacy teams are stretched thin managing dispensing and day-to-day operations. To qualify for Pharmacy First consultation payments, you need to handle at least 25 consultations a month.

That’s where automation and AI make the difference. Tools like Titan streamline workflows, automate admin tasks, and free up your team to focus on patients.

The results:
• More consultations, more revenue.
• Faster, smarter patient care.
• A future-ready pharmacy that thrives under pressure.

With the right systems, you can meet rising demand, grow private services, and keep your pharmacy running smoothly.

How has your pharmacy handled the shift? Let’s discuss in the comments.

posts.idea: DSIC deep dive carousel. Based on Wahid's viral post (127 reactions, 18 comments). Titanverse selected for DSIC Group 1 onboarding. Research-verified: BaRS = Booking and Referral Standard (digital referrals from GPs/111), GP Connect = read/write patient records, MYS = Manage Your Service (NHSBSA claims portal), Pharmacy First = 7 clinical conditions service. Sunny Parmar quote: pharmacists spend 25 mins entering same data across 3 platforms. Guardrail: frame as what DSIC enables, not claim end-to-end support today. 7-8 slides. Cam designs.
posts.post_caption:
Titanverse has been selected for DSIC Group 1 onboarding.

If that sentence means nothing to you, here is why it should.

DSIC is the NHS framework that governs how pharmacy IT systems connect to national services. Safety. Security. Interoperability. Without it, your clinical system cannot talk to the rest of the NHS properly.

Right now, most pharmacists enter the same patient data across three separate platforms. NHS systems. PharmOutcomes. Their PMR. The same consultation. Three times. Twenty-five minutes of duplication per service.

DSIC onboarding means Titanverse is being built to connect directly to:

BaRS, so referrals from GPs and NHS 111 arrive inside your clinical workflow, not in an email.

GP Connect, so you can read a patient's GP record during a consultation and write the summary back automatically. No letter. No NHSmail.

MYS, so service claims submit from the same system you documented the consultation in. Not a separate portal at month end.

Pharmacy First and 111 referrals, so the digital pathway runs end to end without manual handoffs.

This is not a feature announcement. It is infrastructure.

The pharmacies that will benefit most from Pharmacy First, IP services and NHS clinical pathways are the ones whose systems were built to handle them from the start.

That is what DSIC onboarding means.

#Titanverse #DSIC #NHSIntegration #PharmacyFirst #CommunityPharmacy #GPConnect

TITANUP SPONSOR SPOTLIGHT — Tue 2 June 13:00 BST. LIGHT slot. The Sublime Group sponsor announcement. FINAL sponsor reveal — event eve, 5 days out.

WHY THIS SLOT: Tue 13:00 (lunchtime) is the chosen slot for the final sponsor reveal — sits between the DemDx Wed-3-June reveal and the event itself (Sat 7 June). One day's gap from DemDx so it doesn't crowd. Lunchtime not 07:00 because event-eve reveal benefits from being seen during the workday rather than the morning commute, and to differentiate it from the Wed pattern. LIGHT classification stands.

ORDER RATIONALE: Brief received 24 Apr (last of the six). Sublime are the physical-build sponsor (dispensary furniture, shopfitting) — distinct lane from all the others. Final reveal makes sense because the dispensary fit-out is the physical layer that pharmacy owners walking into TitanUp on June 7th will be thinking about — what does the actual room look like.

CAPTION PATTERN: Cliffhanger reveal opener. Lean into the manufacturing pedigree — Kent factory, in-house production, CNC capability. Concrete details land harder than 'we make furniture'.

CAPTION ANGLE TO EXPLORE: All the software in the world doesn't help if your dispensary is the wrong shape. Physical workflow is the layer underneath the digital one. Sublime are the people who build the space all the other sponsor tools run inside. That's the angle worth landing for the final reveal — completing the picture of TitanUp.

The Sublime Group manufacture furniture for Pharmacies in-house operating from a fully equipped factory in Kent. Continued investment in latest manufacturing allows Sublime full flexibility in production from our fully CNC machinery allowing us to produce cost effective solutions. posts.post_caption:
DemDx is sponsoring TitanUp 26.

Here is why that matters.

Pharmacy First gave pharmacists the authority to treat seven clinical conditions.

The authority was not the hard part.

The hard part is standing in front of a patient with an unfamiliar presentation and making the right call. Confidently. Consistently. With the documentation to prove it.

Structured, symptom-led pathways aligned with national guidance. Supporting more than 500 conditions. Designed to help pharmacists identify red flags, document care properly, and make better referral decisions.

Not replacing clinical judgement. Supporting it. At the point of care. When it counts.

Come and see it in Birmingham. June 7th.

posts.idea:
TIKTOK PBC CAROUSEL — Sat 25 Apr 11:00 BST. 7-slide pain-stack → reveal → CTA, matching the format Cam referenced from the Leicester dinner event (candid photo + large white text-block overlay at top, black type).

NARRATIVE ARC:
Slides 1-5: Stack of pains that every pharmacy owner feels and heard articulated at PBC last week.
Slide 6: Reveal the source — 'Pharmacy Business Conference 2026'.
Slide 7: CTA to caption for what has to change.

DESIGN SPEC (per reference): vertical 4:5 or 9:16 crop. Large rounded white text-block overlay at top of each image, black sans-serif (looks like SF Pro Display or similar). Text 2 lines max, stays under 12 words. Photo fills the rest.

--- SLIDE-BY-SLIDE ---

SLIDE 1 — HOOK / PAIN 1
Image: IMG_9651 (Titan stand with Titanverse banner, owners looking at screen, dramatic window light — contemplative)
Text: 'Margins keep getting smaller.'

SLIDE 2 — UNIVERSAL
Image: IMG_9600 (two people deep in conversation, Titan hoodie visible in background)
Text: 'If you're in pharmacy, this probably sounds familiar.'

SLIDE 3 — PAIN 2
Image: IMG_9623 (three at cocktail table, serious discussion)
Text: 'Suppliers keep moving. Prices keep jumping.'

SLIDE 4 — PAIN 3
Image: IMG_9674 (single visitor at Titan stand, head-down focused conversation)
Text: 'And the pharmacist is still stuck in the dispensary.'

SLIDE 5 — PAIN 4 (visual irony)
Image: IMG_9587 (Nahria at Titan stand with Titanverse pull-up banner visible: 'The everything app for Pharmacy services')
Text: 'Everyone knows the future is private. Few have the infrastructure.'
(Note: deliberate visual irony — text calls out missing infrastructure, banner behind subtly names the answer)

SLIDE 6 — REVEAL
Image: IMG_9578 (PBC main stage — 'Pharmacy Business Conference 10 Years / Reimagining Pharmacy / Good morning')
Text: 'We heard all of this at Pharmacy Business Conference 2026.'

SLIDE 7 — CTA
Image: IMG_9720 (Titan team member pushing trolley with Hannspree hardware boxes outside venue — forward motion, we came to work)
Text: 'Read the caption for what has to change.'

--- CAPTION BELOW ---

ASSETS: All 7 images in PBC folder (user uploads /mnt/user-data/uploads/PBC.zip). Cam to design in Figma/Canva: portrait crop, text overlay per reference format.

PLATFORMS: TIKTOK@titanpmr + YT@titanpmr + IN@titanpmr + FB@TITAN PMR (all set).

WHY SAT 25 APR: clean weekend slot before HOD TikTok weekend cadence starts 3 May. PBC carousel gets its own window. Also, posting 10 days after PBC keeps the event reference timely.
posts.post_caption:
The direction of travel is obvious.

Margins are tighter than last year. Suppliers are less reliable. The pharmacist is still doing the dispensary's work instead of the work they trained for.

Every pharmacy owner at the Pharmacy Business Conference last week was saying the same thing.

The future is private services. Everyone knows it. The NHS isn't coming back for us.

Knowing is one thing. Building for it is another.

A pharmacy running one private service at a time can get away with duct tape. A pharmacy running ten needs infrastructure.

That is the whole question now.

Titan carries dispensing at scale. Titanverse runs services without the fragmentation. TitanUp 26 is where we are putting it all in one room, with the people already doing it.

Birmingham. June 7th.

titanup.club

#TitanUp26 #CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyBusiness

posts.post_caption:
The companies building the future of pharmacy.

Drug Comparison.

Buying pressure is the quiet killer in community pharmacy.

Prices move daily. Suppliers move stock unpredictably. Concession lists arrive late. Margins keep tightening and the work to hold them keeps getting harder.

Most pharmacies are making these decisions with yesterday's data and a gut feel.

Drug Comparison fixed that.

The UK's largest independent ordering and buying optimisation platform, built by a pharmacy owner who knew the problem first-hand. Used by nearly 3,000 pharmacies — from single independents to multi-site groups — to order smarter across every supplier and surface the data that drives real savings.

Newer modules go further. DC Warehouse lets groups buy centrally and supply their own branches. DC Market Intel runs live price and stock monitoring.

Not theory. A tool built inside the problem it solves.

Drug Comparison will be at TitanUp 26. Come and see how your buying decisions could be different.

www.titanpmr.com

#TitanUp26 #CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyBuying #IndependentPharmacy

posts.idea:
PMR MEME — Wed 22 Apr. Fills the weekly meme requirement (strategy: 1 meme minimum per week).

Angle: Pharmacy First cap trap. Pharmacies that grew their delivery in 2024/25 now have caps set based on that higher volume. Roll-over of unspent allocation from 2024/25 not permitted into 2025/26. The harder you worked, the higher your target. Above cap = no fee.

Format: Two Buttons meme template. Not used before (existing memes: battery level, This Is Fine, Drake, Distracted Boyfriend, 4-panel procrastination, Gandalf, LOTR, printing tokens, private service 9am).

Design brief:
Template: Two Buttons (pharmacist sweating between two red buttons)
Button 1 label: 'Grow your Pharmacy First delivery'
Button 2 label: 'Your cap this year is based on last year’s delivery'
Pharmarist label: 'You'
Bottom caption strip: 'TITAN PMR' logo bottom right, dark background strip
Tone: Wry. Accurate. Pharmacy owners will tag each other.

Caption: Short. Under 50 words. Let the image work.

═══════════════════════════════════════════
HANDOFF — LOCKED (19 Apr)
═══════════════════════════════════════════

Format: 1080 × 1080 (square meme standard)
Template: Two Buttons — classic two red buttons with sweating figure above

ON-IMAGE TEXT (locked):

BUTTON 1 (left, red):
GROW YOUR PHARMACY FIRST DELIVERY

BUTTON 2 (right, red):
ACCEPT A CAP BASED ON LAST YEAR'S DELIVERY

FIGURE LABEL (white label on sweating figure, stroked for legibility):
You

BOTTOM BAR (dark strip across bottom):
Titan PMR logo right-aligned. Nothing else.

═══════════════════════════════════════════
ASSET NEEDED FROM DESIGNER
═══════════════════════════════════════════
• Two Buttons source image (widely available meme template). Do NOT edit or distort the original photo — just overlay the button labels and figure label.
• Titan PMR logo lockup — use existing brand asset.

No stock licensing issue with meme template — it's a standard format. But keep it respectful in tone: this is wry observation, not cynicism.

═══════════════════════════════════════════
FACT-CHECK STATUS (19 Apr)
═══════════════════════════════════════════
Claim in caption: 'your cap this year is based on last year's delivery', 'above your cap = no fee'.

STATUS: NEEDS VERIFICATION BEFORE PUBLISH

The cap-setting methodology and no-roll-over rules vary by service year. Before this meme goes live, verify against:
• CPE Pharmacy First payment guidance (cpe.org.uk)
• NHSBSA Pharmacy First payment FAQs

If cap methodology differs from framing, rewrite Button 2. The meme format is strong; the factual framing just needs to be airtight before publish. This is a reactive news-style post so accuracy matters more than the comedy.
posts.post_caption:
You grew your Pharmacy First delivery last year.

Your cap this year: based on last year's actual delivery.

The more you delivered, the more they expected.

Above your cap? No fee.

#TitanPMR #CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyFirst #CPCF
posts.name: TITAN_PharmacyFirstCapTrap_Meme

posts.idea:
TITANVERSE POLICY-TO-PRACTICE CAROUSEL — PULLED FORWARD from Tue 28 Apr to Wed 22 Apr 07:00 BST to rescue the Titanverse gap this week. 8 slides. Carousel is the source of truth — this brief reflects the final signed-off asset.

NARRATIVE ARC:
Hook (prescribers coming, infrastructure not) → Date (Sept 2026) → Proof (pathfinder model worked) → Outcome stats (59%, 97%) → Twist (93% IPs blocked) → Pain (fragmentation reality) → CTA question.

SLIDE-BY-SLIDE:

Slide 1 — Hook
The prescribers are coming.
The infrastructure isn't.
Visual: stethoscope with severed, sparking cable on dark purple cosmic background.

Slide 2 — Timing
SEPTEMBER 2026
Every newly qualified pharmacist joins the register as an independent prescriber.
Visual: torn calendar page, September 2026.

Slide 3 — Proof the model works
THE MODEL ALREADY WORKED
33,000+ consultations
164 pharmacy sites
Visual: grid of consultation record cards fading into a light trail.

Slide 4 — Outcome stat 1
59%
led to prescribing interventions that would otherwise have needed a GP or hospital
Visual: pink-to-purple gradient number, data waveform beneath.

Slide 5 — Outcome stat 2
97%
of episodes closed in community pharmacy without onward referral
Visual: same gradient stat treatment, energy pulse beneath.

Slide 6 — The twist
93%
of IPs in community pharmacy can't use what they trained for
Not because they can't prescribe. But because the infrastructure won't let them.
Visual: sparks and broken cable fragment — echoes slide 1 visual motif, closes the loop.

Slide 7 — The reality
Bullet list:
- Multiple platforms that don't talk to each other
- Duplicate data entry
- Limited patient record access
- Clinical governance dependent on workarounds
Visual: pharmacist in dim consultation room surrounded by cluttered screens, visibly frustrated.

Slide 8 — CTA
Is your pharmacy building for prescribing?
Titanverse logo anchor, no sparkles.

FACT-CHECK STATUS (verified 19 Apr 2026):
✔ 33,000 consultations / 164 sites — NHS England letter to ICBs, Oct 2025
✔ 59% prescribing intervention rate — same NHS England letter
✔ 97% closed without referral — Pathfinder national evaluation, reported by Pharmaceutical Journal and The Pharmacist (Jan 2026)
✔ 93% community pharmacy IPs not using IP qualification — NHS England Community Pharmacy Workforce Survey (July 2025), reported by Pharmaceutical Journal (Feb 2026). Framing specifically scoped to community pharmacy to match the source.

posts.idea:
REACTIVE INDUSTRY CAROUSEL — Mon 4 May 07:00 BST. HEAVY slot. Reframed from the original CPCF carousel (which was scheduled Mon 27 Apr but dropped). News has moved on — the 14x medicine pricing story is now the bigger, more current beat. CPCF folded in as a supporting beat in slide 4, not the headline.

ANGLE: The cost pressure has moved from the funding settlement (abstract, Westminster) to the wholesaler invoice (concrete, this morning). The story is what it costs to dispense one item right now.

FRESHNESS NOTE: Mon 4 May is the early May bank holiday. Reactive content takes Monday morning per strategy 5.9. If audience size matters more than freshness, can move to Tue 5 May 07:00 — flag this with Hooman before publish.

6-slide carousel:
Slide 1 — Hook: 'Pharmacies are paying 14× more for some medicines than they did in January.' Statement, no question. Tier 1 hook pattern.
Slide 2 — The numbers: paracetamol 41p → £1.99 (peak end of March, since dropped to £1.09). Cetirizine pack nearly doubled. Some distributors reportedly charging £3 a pack.
Slide 3 — Why: global manufacturers prioritising markets that pay more. UK reimbursement not keeping up. NPA has called on Government to put more money in to guarantee supply.
Slide 4 — The compounding pressure: CPCF 2026/27 still unsettled (day 35 of new financial year by Mon 5 May). Feb 2026 price concessions hit a three-year high (174 lines). One in four pharmacy teams spending 2hr+ a day sourcing alternatives.
Slide 5 — What pharmacies absorb: when reimbursement does not cover sourcing cost, the difference comes off margin. On every single line. Every day.
Slide 6 — The shift: pharmacies holding ground are reducing how much of the pharmacist's day goes into routine checking, and growing the services revenue line that has no margin pressure. Titan branding lands here only.

Design: Dark. Bold type. Stats as hero elements. Same visual language as original CPCF brief — no rebuild on design side, only copy. No Titan branding until slide 6.

FACT-CHECK STATUS (verified 28 Apr 2026):
✔ 14-15x medicine pricing — NPA chair Oliver Picard, reported in Pharmacy Magazine, BBC Radio 4, The Guardian (April 2026). Quote framed in third person to respect copyright + brand voice.
✔ Paracetamol 41p → £1.99 — Picard/Guardian (April 2026), since dropped to £1.09. Use the 41p → £1.99 frame for narrative impact, mention the drop only if needed for accuracy.
✔ Cetirizine 30-pack ~37p, distributors reportedly £3 — Picard/Guardian (April 2026). Phrase as 'reportedly' on the £3 figure since it's distributor-level not market-wide.
✔ CPCF 2026/27 not agreed — CPE confirmed 27 March 2026; still unresolved at 28 Apr. Source: Chemist+Druggist.
✔ 174 lines / three-year high — Pharmaceutical Journal, Feb 2026 (highest since March 2023's 178).
✔ One in four spending 2hr+/day on sourcing — CPE Pharmacy Pressures Survey Nov 2025.

IMPARTIALITY: No favouritism toward specific wholesalers. No NHS body tagged for endorsement reasons.

PER MEMORY: No em dashes. No pride language. British English. Approved emoji set only. 'Rollout' as one word. Picard's quote paraphrased in third person, not lifted verbatim.
posts.post_caption:
Pharmacies are paying 14× more for some medicines than they did in January.

Not all of them. But enough that it changes the maths.

Paracetamol. 100 pack. 41p in January. £1.99 by the end of March.
Cetirizine. 30 pack. Nearly doubled. Some distributors reportedly charging £3.

Why?

Global manufacturers price by market. The UK pays one of the lowest rates in Europe. When supply tightens, stock goes where the return is better.

Reimbursement doesn't move at the same speed.

So pharmacies absorb the difference. On every line. Every day.

This is happening alongside everything else.

The 2026/27 contract is still not agreed. Day 35 of the new financial year.
Price concessions hit a three-year high in February. 174 products pharmacies couldn't source at Drug Tariff price.
One in four pharmacy teams now spend more than two hours a day sourcing alternatives.

This is not one bad month. It is the structural reality of running a community pharmacy in 2026.

The pharmacies holding ground are not waiting for the contract to fix it.

They are reducing how much of the pharmacist's day goes into routine checking.
They are running services that aren't capped against last year's delivery.
They are getting more clinical output from the same team.

Because that is the only revenue line that doesn't get squeezed by the next price spike.

👇 What's the most painful line on your purchase invoice this month?

#TitanPMR #CommunityPharmacy #MedicineSupply #PharmacyFunding #CPCF #NHSPharmacy #PharmacyBusiness
posts.name: TITAN_MedicinePricing_Reactive

posts.idea:
INDUSTRY STANCE CAROUSEL — Week of 11 May, counterweight to meme on Mon 11 May.

Angle: 'The NHS wants pharmacists to prescribe. But wanting it and funding the infrastructure are two different things.'

This is the natural follow-through from the meme. Meme lands the joke. This post lands the argument.

Architecture (6 slides):
Slide 1 — Hook: 'The NHS is producing thousands of independent prescribers. Most of them still cannot prescribe in practice.'
Slide 2 — The gap: trained, motivated, registered. But the dispensary queue eats the morning before a single consultation starts.
Slide 3 — The actual blocker: it is not skills. It is not will. It is workflow. The pharmacist is still the bottleneck in every check.
Slide 4 — What pharmacy needs vs. what it has. Clinical capacity vs. manual process overhead.
Slide 5 — The mechanism: what changes when AI clinical checking absorbs the routine. The pharmacist stops being the funnel.
Slide 6 — Close: 'Training more prescribers is not the answer. Freeing the ones you already have is.'

CTA: Engagement question. 'How many IPs in your pharmacy actually get to prescribe regularly? Drop a number below.'

Update: Check C+D and PJ for any fresh IP or NHS workforce angle in early May and fold in the specific news hook before publishing.

Design: Dark backgrounds, bold white type. No Titan branding until slide 6.
posts.post_caption:
The NHS is producing thousands of independent prescribers.

Most of them still cannot prescribe.

Not because they are unwilling.
Not because the training is wrong.

Because the dispensary queue still eats their morning.

Before a single consultation starts, the pharmacist is already three hours into the check stack.

The NHS 10-year plan wants pharmacy at the centre of clinical care.

But wanting it and funding the infrastructure for it are not the same thing.

The pharmacists are trained.
The will is there.
The workflow is not.

Fix the workflow. Free the prescriber.

👇 How many IPs in your pharmacy actually get to prescribe regularly? Drop a number below.

#TitanPMR #CommunityPharmacy #IndependentPrescribing #PharmacyWorkflow #NHSPharmacy #PharmacyFirst

Format options:
1. Distracted Boyfriend — Pharmacist (labelled 'me, IP qualified') turning away from 'morning prescribing clinic' to look at 'the dispensary queue that needs clearing first'
2. Two Buttons — Pharmacist sweating between 'run the prescribing clinic' and 'check these 200 items before anyone else can'
3. Drake format — NHS training thousands of IPs (bottom panel: approved) / giving them the time and workflow to actually prescribe (top panel: nope)

Caption: Short. 1-2 lines max. Let the image work. See Post Caption field for draft.

Tone: Wry, not angry. Pharmacists laugh at this because it is true. Not a complaint post — an observation post.

Function: Reach + follower acquisition. IP angle is timely (NHS 10-year plan, prescribing pilot expansion). Strong share and tag mechanic.

posts.post_caption:
Private care gets talked about like strategy.

But strategy does not run a consultation.

A workflow does.

Booking.
Documentation.
Prescribing.
Payment.

posts.idea:
7-slide carousel — The Relationship That Never Happened
Focus: Private care economics and CRM

OPEN LOOP MECHANISM: Slide 1 opens the loop with a specific, recognisable scenario — private patient pays, leaves, never returns. The loop is: why does that keep happening, and what changes it? Titanverse arrives on slide 6 as the infrastructure that closes the gap, not as a product pitch.

---

SLIDE 1 — Hook
"A patient pays £55 for a private weight management consultation."
"They leave."
"You never speak to them again."
Three lines. Each on its own row. Generous space between them. The third line lands as the problem — the reader asks: is that wrong? Swipe to find out.
Background: near-black (#1a1a1a). Anton, white.

SLIDE 2 — Reframe
"That is not a bad outcome."
"That is the default."
Small header: "In most pharmacies, a private consultation ends when the patient walks out."
No follow-up pathway. No recall system. No next appointment.
Background: near-black. This slide reframes the problem as structural, not personal. The reader recognises their own operation.

SLIDE 3 — Contrast
Split layout. Two columns:
Left: "Other clinical settings" — care record, follow-up booked, reason to return.
Right: "Pharmacy" — transaction ends at the counter.
Same patient. Different infrastructure. Different outcome.
Background: near-black. The visual contrast IS the argument.

SLIDE 4 — Why it happens
"That is not a patient care problem."
"It is a systems problem."
The tools most pharmacies use for private services were not built to track the patient beyond the appointment.
Background: near-black. Blame the infrastructure, not the pharmacist.

SLIDE 5 — The value argument (no fabricated stats)
Three parallel statements in sequence:
"A patient who comes back for review is more valuable than a new one."
"A patient who books travel vaccines because someone followed up is more valuable than a new one."
"A patient who hears from you before their annual review is more valuable than a new one."
All three require a system that owns the relationship after the consultation.
Background: near-black shifting to gradient. The rhythm of the three statements builds weight.

SLIDE 6 — Mechanism reveal
"When follow-up, re-booking triggers and long-term care tracking are built into the same platform as the consultation, the relationship does not end at the door."
"It continues because the system continues it."
Background: full Titanverse gradient (#FF65DD to #B752FF). This is the earned reveal.

SLIDE 7 — Takeaway
"Private services are not just about the appointment."
"They are about whether the patient comes back."
"That only happens when something is looking after the relationship."
Background: Titanverse gradient. One thought per line. Restrained. No hard sell.

---

DESIGN RULES:
— Anton for all statement slides
— SF Pro for supporting text
— Max 15 words on image per slide
— Near-black (#1a1a1a) slides 1—5
— Titanverse gradient (#FF65DD to #B752FF) slides 6—7
— Slide 3 split layout is the visual centrepiece — give it room
— No fabricated statistics anywhere
— No Titanverse logo on image
— 1:1 square aspect ratio
posts.post_caption:
A patient pays for a private weight management consultation.
They leave.
You never speak to them again.

That is not a failure. That is the default.

In most pharmacies, the private consultation ends the moment the patient walks out. No follow-up. No recall. No reason built into the system for them to return.

In any other clinical setting, that patient would have a care record, a follow-up booked, and a pathway back to you.

A returning patient costs less to retain than a new one costs to acquire. Every private service you offer is a potential long-term clinical relationship. Most pharmacies are leaving that on the table because the tools they use were not built to own what happens after the appointment.

#Titanverse #PrivateHealthcare #PharmacyServices #PharmacyBusiness

posts.idea:
6-slide carousel — Data & Reporting theme

Slide 1: Bold hook — "WHAT IF YOUR PMR COULD ANSWER THE QUESTIONS THAT ACTUALLY MATTER?" Dark background, large white text.

Slide 2: "Not this:" with typical dashboard metrics: fridge log tick, CD register tick, barcode %. Boring checklist.

Slide 3: "But this:" with real owner questions:
- How much money did I make this month?
- Which branch is losing me money?
- Am I GPhC ready right now?
- Where are my patients coming from?

Slide 4: "The old way" — pie charts, bar graphs that look busy but say nothing.

Slide 5: "The Titan way" — Question-driven analytics. You ask, Titan answers.

Slide 6: CTA — "Data should answer questions, not create more." Titan PMR logo. titanpmr.com
posts.post_caption:
Your PMR gives you 50 graphs.

But can it answer one simple question?

"How much money did I make this month at my Oakley branch?"

Most dashboards show you fridge logs, barcode percentages, CD registers.
Tick, tick, tick.

Nobody's lying awake at 2am thinking about their fridge log.

They're thinking:
• Am I losing money?
• Can I afford to hire?
• If GPhC walked in tomorrow, am I ready?

Data should answer questions. Not create more of them.

👉 titanpmr.com

#TitanPMR #PharmacyData #PharmacyAnalytics #CommunityPharmacy

posts.idea:
Single image. Dark background. Titanverse gradient accents (#FF65DD to #B752FF).

Visualise the gap, not the solution. Three stages of a patient journey laid out horizontally:

CONSULTATION — [VOID] — NEXT APPOINTMENT

Stage one (Consultation) is solid, lit, real. The middle section is the visual tension: a long empty space with a broken line, labelled simply — 'This is where patients disappear.' Stage three (Next Appointment) is faded, ghosted — uncertain.

No emoji. No bullet points. Pure typography and geometry. The emptiness is the point.

Small Titanverse logo bottom right. No CTA on the image — that is tomorrow's post's job.
posts.post_caption:
The consultation went well.

The patient left.

Then nothing.

No confirmation message.
No follow-up.
No pre-consult before the next appointment.

Pharmacy puts everything into getting patients through the door. Then hands them off to silence.

That is not continuity of care. That is a one-touch service dressed up as something more.

The value of a clinical service is not the consultation.

It is everything that happens around it.

#Titanverse #ClinicalPharmacy #CommunityPharmacy #PatientCare #PharmacyServices

posts.idea:
Rahul Titanverse Transformation — Longform Video, 2-3 minutes

---
EDITOR REFERENCE — FULL STRUCTURE WITH TIMESTAMPS:

0:00–0:15 HOOK
Timestamp 9:09–9:15
"Honestly, I can't believe that AI transcription wasn't a thing in pharmacy before."
(Clean hook take, straight to camera. Rehearsed delivery.)

0:15–0:45 THE BEFORE — ProScript & the old world
Timestamp 1:53–2:56
"We got two pharmacies. We were using ProScript... ProScript is quite archaic. It requires you printing off a batch of prescriptions, loads of labels, but it just slowed your whole process down. Baskets were everywhere. Pharmacists had to be actively involved in checking, and it meant that pharmacists didn't have enough time to do the majority of their NHS services, or even start to think about private services."

Also weave in (6:13–7:07):
"Writing notes would have been on scraps of paper... you're either typing something, not looking at the patient, or you're scribbling things down... note taking was, in fairness, pretty poor."

0:45–1:30 THE SHIFT — Titanverse changes everything
Timestamp 9:15–9:38 (continuation of hook):
"As soon as we were aware that something had existed in a PMR system, it meant that we could create a safe system. Didn't have to think about using another software, copying and pasting or uploading it elsewhere. It was all in one software."

Timestamp 11:36–12:37:
"What we didn't realize with Titanverse was how everything was under one roof. We could send a message to a patient to confirm their appointment... questionnaires were built into it... it meant that we could create a far more streamlined service... Previously we might have thought we could only do five consultations in an hour, and now we can do more."

Timestamp 12:37–13:10:
"Titanverse is a game changer. Having something linked to your PMR means you don't have to have multiple tabs open. You have it all under one system. And the fact that it's web based, if I'm in one branch I can access the information for a patient at my other branch."

1:30–2:00 THE PROOF — How it works day to day
Timestamp 13:24–14:29:
Rahul walks through the actual consultation flow: pre-consult questionnaires sent via SMS, clinician reviews before appointment, AI transcription runs during consult, summary generated, full transcript stored, vitals recorded, prescription sent to Titan for dispensing.

Timestamp 19:45–20:15:
"Multiple hours a day... we've been able to probably double the amount of consultations that we're doing. The standard of our notes is completely improved."

Timestamp 21:13–21:50:
"We've seen that we can now offer twice as many consults, and that's kind of proof in what we're doing."

2:00–2:30 THE INDUSTRY VIEW — Why this matters now
Timestamp 24:02–24:52:
"Long gone are the days that pharmacies should be regarded as just dispensing outlets... funding is completely pulling the rug underneath pharmacies... now thinking about offering private services but high standard and high quality of care... we now use systems like Titan and Titanverse to deliver this in a digital age where patients' expectations are at the highest."

Timestamp 28:37–29:22:
"People still regard pharmacies as chemists. We're now clinical diagnostic hubs. We're supporting people through whether it be the NHS arm or the private arm."

2:30–2:45 THE CLOSE
Timestamp 37:47:
"Pharmacy will not survive if you just do NHS services in 2026."

End card: Titanverse logo + titanverse.co.uk

---
BONUS SECTIONS (use if extending or for B-roll narration):

DemDx integration (16:30–19:30): Rahul explains DemDx clinical pathways, pharmacy first PGDs, clinical calculators. Very detailed, good for an integration-specific cut.

Templates (21:55–22:40): Custom template building in Titanverse for common conditions and continuous care patients.

Pharmacy renovation (23:16–24:00): Renovated pharmacy specifically for services, two consultation rooms, wouldn't have done it without switching to Titan/Titanverse.

Scaling advice (39:39–43:30): "Being impatient and not investing in systems" — research your market, each private service is its own business, pharmacy undersells itself. Very quotable.

TitanUp tease (46:35–47:08): "If an opportunity like TitanUp came to connect everyone... to discuss the future of pharmacy, then of course we'll be there. It's now about collaborating and working with one another."
posts.post_caption:
"Honestly, I can't believe AI transcription wasn't a thing in pharmacy before."

Rahul runs two branches. 30-50 consultations a week.

Before Titanverse:
• Notes on scraps of paper
• Typing instead of looking at the patient
• Five tabs open, one always timing out
• Pharmacist torn between bench and consultation room

After Titanverse:
• AI transcribes the entire consultation
• 10-20 minutes saved per patient
• Everything compliant, nothing missed
• Pharmacist fully in the consultation room

"Titanverse is a game changer."

🎬 Watch the full story.

Sometimes the best proof isn't a demo.

It's what happens when you take it away.

#TitanPMR #StockControl #PharmacyEfficiency

posts.idea:
Carousel Script (7 slides):
SLIDE 1 (Hook):

£158 MILLION CLAWBACK

Announced Dec 23rd.
Two days before Christmas.
Quietly slipped out.

Here's what you need to know.
SLIDE 2 (What is clawback?):

WHAT IS CLAWBACK?

The CPCF sets an "allowed profit envelope" for pharmacy.

If the sector's profit exceeds this level, the government claws it back the following year.

Sounds fair?

Keep reading.
SLIDE 3 (The unfair bit):

HERE'S THE CATCH

Clawback is calculated on the WHOLE INDUSTRY.

Not individual pharmacies.

If Boots makes excess profit, your pharmacy pays too.

Even if you're dispensing at a loss.
SLIDE 4 (The numbers):

THE REALITY

- £16.8m clawed back per quarter
- Starting January 2026
- Applied to Category M and A drugs
- No individual assessment
- No appeals process
SLIDE 5 (The context):

THE BIGGER PICTURE

- 63% of pharmacies at risk of closure
- 248 closed in 2024 alone
- <10,000 remain in England
- 60% of owners took no salary in 2024
- £2.6bn funding gap

And now: clawback.
SLIDE 6 (The quote):

"Pharmacies cannot plan, cannot invest, and are forced to dispense NHS medicines, day after day, often at a loss."

– Olivier Picard, NPA Chair
SLIDE 7 (CTA):

WHAT CAN YOU DO?

1. Understand your exposure
2. Track affected drug categories
3. Make the opaque visible

Titan can help.

titanpmr.com
Visual Brief:
• Bold, stark design
• Use red/warning colour accents
• Large numbers on each slide
• Clean, impactful typography
• NPA quote on dedicated slide
• Titan positioning subtle - focused on industry advocacy first
Notes:
• This is "editorial/oracle" positioning - Titan being the voice of pharmacy
• Pairs anger/injustice with practical help (reporting)
• Follows the Mounjaro pricing post formula (timely + affects wallets + emotional)
• Can link to a future "how Titan helps with clawback tracking" follow-up post

posts.post_caption:
Bulk nomination is undermining patient choice.

And it has created a completely unfair playing field.

Some PMR systems allow pharmacies to switch patients back in bulk, without consent and without a conversation. Patients have no idea their nomination has changed behind their backs.

Pharmacies who refuse to do this lose business.
Pharmacies who do it gain business.
That isn’t fair competition. It’s a broken system.

Bulk switching goes directly against patient choice.
And it punishes the pharmacies who try to do the right thing.

There are only two legitimate scenarios for a bulk nomination change:
• When a pharmacy is sold
• When two pharmacies merge
And even then, the NHS manages the change — not the PMR system.

Everywhere else, nomination should stay where it belongs:
with the patient.

If you agree this needs to stop, take a stand with us.

👉 Sign the petition: bit.ly/3YH8boA
posts.name: Bulk nomination is undermining patient choice.

posts.post_caption:
Safy’s Chemist was losing time every single day.

They were handling 14,500 to 15,000 items a month, and they felt the pace.

Baskets piling up.
Running between rooms.
Checking, bagging and shelving in between appointments.
Back and forth all day.

For Asif, that rhythm felt normal for a busy pharmacy.

Titan changed that completely.

He now spends about an hour in the dispensary across a full day.
Around 85 percent of his time is spent outside it.
The team runs the process.
He can sit with patients.
People mention it in their reviews ⭐

Safety changed as well.
On ProScript, they had five or six near misses a month.
Since moving to Titan, they have had two in an entire year.
Both were human errors, not system issues.

As Asif puts it:
“Now I have zero worries. Honestly, it’s the best thing that’s happened.”

His story shows something many pharmacies overlook.
You are losing more time than you think.
Once that changes, everything else follows.

📖 Read the full case study → http://bit.ly/4sB6bMl

#TitanPMR #PharmacyWorkflow #PharmacySafety #PharmacyInnovation #CommunityPharmacy #IndependentPharmacy

posts.idea:
SLIDE 1 — HOOK

Manual checking works.
Until scale makes it unsafe.

⸻

SLIDE 2 — THE BEHAVIOUR

Why does manual double-checking exist?

Because verification isn’t enforced at the point of action.
So people compensate.

⸻

SLIDE 3 — THE FAILURE

This works until volume breaks it.

Vigilance degrades faster than workload increases.

⸻

SLIDE 4 — THE COST

The cost lands on cognitive load.

Mental energy goes into catching errors
instead of overseeing care.

⸻

SLIDE 5 — WHAT FAILS FIRST

Dispensing stability breaks first.

When safety depends on human presence,
scale becomes a risk.

⸻

SLIDE 6 — CLOSE

Systems should absorb complexity.
People shouldn’t.
posts.post_caption:
Manual verification feels responsible.
Until it becomes the bottleneck.

People double-check because the system lets work move forward without confirmation.
That’s fine at low volume.

At scale, repetition erodes attention.
Safety becomes probabilistic.
And throughput is gated by one human checkpoint.

The cost doesn’t show up as failure straight away.
It shows up as cognitive load.

Stable dispensing isn’t about asking people to focus harder.
It’s about building systems that don’t rely on focus at all.

posts.post_caption:
Partial systems create full-scale problems.

Human triage burns people out. Digital triage scales 🔄

In most pharmacies, people are the system.
They carry the priority 🧠

When the system cannot surface what matters, everything turns into ad hoc triage.

One person decides what is urgent.
One person remembers what is next.
One person holds the risk ⚠️

It works for a while.
Then the volume hits.

Interruptions spike.
Signals get missed.
Fatigue creeps in 😮‍💨

It does not fail instantly.
It fails at scale.

The risk does not disappear.
It lands on the people trying to hold everything together.

And when complexity keeps landing on humans, the cost shows up fast:

• throughput drops ⏳
• stress climbs 😵‍💫
• confidence slips
• growth stalls 📉

You cannot try harder out of this.

You need systems that think before people have to.

When priority and logic live inside the infrastructure, not in someone’s head, you stop relying on heroics and start building something that can actually scale 💡

See how TITAN removes the hero burden → http://bit.ly/3Nk6VW2

#PharmacyInnovation #DigitalPharmacy #PharmacyWorkflow #AIinHealthcare #TitanPMR

Independent Prescribing should be the future.

So why will it struggle in 2026?

Every branch is training more IPs. Every policy document points to a more clinical role. But in reality, most pharmacists still start their day the same way they did ten years ago: clearing repeats, chasing stock and firefighting the bench.

Pharmacy First showed the demand is there.
What is missing is capacity.

Training more prescribers cannot overcome a workflow that gives them no time to prescribe.

Until the operational model changes, IP cannot scale.
The bottleneck is not skill. It is time.

posts.post_caption:
Stop playing Jenga in pharmacy.

Start doing the work that actually matters.

Every owner knows the drill. Chasing baskets. Fixing mis picks. Clearing piles. Checking everything because the system cannot tell you what truly needs you. By the time you look up, half the day has slipped.

Here is what changes when the workflow is built to remove chaos:

• Clinical checks happen upfront
• AI flags the high risk first
• Checking drops from 100 percent to 20 to 30 percent
• Baskets flow instead of blocking the room
• The team can run the queue without you glued to the bench

And suddenly your time goes where it should:

• Pharmacy First
• NMS
• IP clinics
• Vaccinations
• Ear wax
• Weight management
• Real patient conversations

This is what happens when the system lifts the load, not you.

posts.post_caption:
Spot the difference. 

The GTIN doesn’t.

Right now in UK pharmacy, different medicines are arriving with the same GTIN barcode or a GTIN that does not match the product inside.

When that happens, the scanner passes the wrong medicine as correct. Every safety layer collapses.

Pharmacists are forced to slow down, override the check, and carry the risk that should sit with manufacturers and regulators.

This is not a workflow problem. It is a patient safety problem.

The fix is simple:
• Make accurate GTIN barcodes mandatory
• Treat incorrect GTINs as labelling errors
• Protect the final safety check that keeps patients safe

If you work in pharmacy, please add your name.
This is a small action that protects every dispenser in the country.

👉 Sign the petition: bit.ly/3XNxapM

#EveryPackCounts #PharmacySafety #GTIN #CommunityPharmacy #TitanPMR

posts.post_caption:
170,000 items a month sounds impressive.

Living it is something else. 👀

Yusuf knew BMP HEALTHCARE LTD could not keep absorbing that pressure.
Too much checking. Too much noise. Too many days shaped by the bench.

Then he saw Titan at the Pharmacy Show in 2023.
Something clicked. ⚡
Not hype. Not promises.
A clear path to a calmer, faster operation.

So they rebuilt.
Pharmacists moved into consultation rooms.
Scanning became second nature.
Checks fell to around 20 to 30 percent.
Services grew by hundreds of percent.
The pressure lifted. 💬

This is the video where Yusuf talks through that shift.
What changed.
What surprised him.
And why he will not go back.

📖 Full written story → http://bit.ly/4nOpOgu

posts.post_caption:
Inside Tariq’s Pharmacy Show Talk.
The message was clear.
Dispensing alone isn’t enough anymore.

At The Pharmacy Show 2025, Tariq Muhammad shared what many in the room already felt.
The model that once made pharmacy stable is now under strain.

Funding is flat. Volume keeps rising.
But the future isn’t doom and gloom.
It’s in taking control.

From TITAN’s survey of 95 pharmacies:
📊 86% plan to grow private services.
📈 59% still want to expand NHS delivery.

Pharmacy’s direction of travel is clear.
Independent. Efficient. Service-led.

If you missed the talk, this carousel shares the key messages that resonated most.
You can also read the full piece here:
bit.ly/43eKMO2

posts.post_caption:
Hub and spoke. Before the law changes.

Medichem Pharmacy shows how.

Most contractors are waiting for October 2025 to see if hub and spoke will work for independents.
But waiting could mean falling behind.

Jeet Vadodaria did not wait.
At Medichem Pharmacy, he built a central hub, connected his four branches with Titan P2P, and reimagined what his team could do.

The results:
✅ Dispensers four times more efficient
✅ 45% less stockholding across branches
✅ Pharmacists free to focus on services
✅ Patients walk into calmer, safer pharmacies

“Peer to peer is a brand new tool, which Titan has enabled us to use at the minute. And I think it’s amazing, because if you are part of the same group, you could technically consider sending your prescriptions to be dispensed at your other branch.” – Jeet Vadodaria

This October, hub and spoke becomes open to every independent.
Medichem proves it can work, safely and profitably, with patients at the centre.

👇 Will your pharmacy be ready?

📖 Full case study: [insert link]

posts.post_caption:
Sure, Teslas can drive themselves.

But pharmacists cannot swap a pill.

That is where we are in 2025.

Patients are waiting up to three weeks for essential medicines.
Not because shelves are empty.
But because pharmacists are legally blocked from substituting.

The result:
👉 Patients left untreated
👉 Surgeries drowning in calls
👉 Pharmacies stuck in the firing line

The absurdity is obvious.
We trust machines to steer cars through traffic.
But not pharmacists to use their training when a drug is out of stock.

The CCA and others are pushing for substitution rights, deprescribing roles and more front line flexibility. The NHS 10 Year Plan points the same way.

The question is not whether pharmacy is ready.
It is whether the rules will catch up.

#pharmacy #NHS #shortages #patientcare #TitanPMR
posts.name: 3 Weeks No Meds

posts.idea:
Hub and Spoke: What Changes, What To Do, What To Watch



Image text: From 1 Oct 2025, independents can use hubs across ownership lines.
Caption (ally voice):
Here’s the short version for owners:
• Law in force: 1 Oct 2025
• To start: notify your ICB at least 28 days before you begin
• If you notify on 1 Oct and there is no objection, earliest start is 29 Oct 2025
Checklist: hub due-diligence, SOPs, courier SLAs, business continuity, data sharing, audit trail.
Bottom line: plan now. Put patient safety and resilience first.
CTA: What is the one risk you will mitigate first? Add it below so others can sanity-check their plan.
posts.post_caption:
Your pharmacy’s workflow could change forever.

From 1 Oct 2025, independents can use hubs across ownership lines.

Here’s what owners need to know:
1️⃣ Law in force: 1 Oct 2025
2️⃣ Notify your ICB: at least 28 days before you begin
3️⃣ Earliest start: If you notify on 1 Oct and there’s no objection → 29 Oct 2025

Before you begin, check off:

• ✔ Hub due diligence
• ✔ SOPs
• ✔ Courier SLAs
• ✔ Business continuity
• ✔ Data sharing
• ✔ Audit trail

Bottom line:
Plan now. Patient safety and business resilience come first.

👉 What’s the first risk you’ll tackle? Share below so others can sanity-check their plan.
posts.name: Hub-and-Spoke: What Actually Changes on 1 Oct?

posts.idea:
•	Evidence: Reddit threads show loneliness and burnout; pharmacists crave solidarity and shared voice. Zero-tolerance campaigns and closure maps perform well when they feel collective.

•	Sector context: Professional bodies often fail to inspire — leaving room for Titan to play “community advocate.”

•	Role: This turns your campaign from content into cause. It builds belonging. That’s loyalty glue.
posts.name: 01_The Movement (Solidarity & Rallying)

posts.idea:
•	Evidence: Case studies (Prab, Sagar, Akbar) show Titan in action solving chaos, scaling without robots, enabling peer-to-peer.

•	Sector context: Pharmacists want practical fixes, not just commiseration. They’re cynical of pure “PMR feature-speak.”

•	Role: Titan comes in here not as a pitch, but as proof of possibility. It arms the hero with weapons in the fight.
posts.name: 01_The Tools (Titan as Enabler)

posts.idea:
•	Evidence: Hot conversations about hub-and-spoke October 2025 rollout, DSP tightening in June, Pharmacy First thresholds and antibiotics misuse, GP referrals friction.

•	Sector data: Legislation dates, CPE myth-busters, Guardian/Pharmaceutical Journal coverage.

•	Role: This gives you authority. You’re not just empathising; you’re guiding. It also taps into fear of being left behind — a strong motivator.
posts.name: 01_The Shift (Policy & Market Change)

posts.idea:
•	Evidence: Patients consistently rate pharmacists as most accessible/trusted. Independents talk with pride about knowing families for generations.

•	Sector data: NHS and CPE guidance reinforces “patient choice” around nominations, showing community pharmacies’ unique role.

•	Role: This flips the lens from victim to proud protagonist. It tells pharmacists: “what you do is special, and worth protecting.”

posts.post_caption:
In most PMRs, safety depends on signatures and memory.
In Titan, it’s automatic.

Every script has a full digital audit trail:
✅ Who clinically checked it
✅ When it was labelled
✅ When it was handed out
✅ Who handed it out

No guesswork. No missing scripts. No “did we give that out?” moments.

For teams, it means less stress and fewer mistakes.
For pharmacists, it means freedom to focus on services.
For patients, it means faster, safer handouts.

That’s what pharmacy should feel like. Calm. Clear. Accountable.

👉 Follow us for more ways Titan is changing pharmacy.

#CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyOwner #FutureOfPharmacy #DigitalPharmacy #TitanPMR #AIinHealthcare

posts.idea:
Meme Idea (detailed execution)


• Format: Bold text-first meme.
• Visual concept:

    ◦ Large central text: “What would you do with 10 free hours a week?”
    ◦ Background: clean clock face (not cheesy stock — elegant, minimalist) or abstract time-lapse motion blur to signal “time regained.”

• Design style:

    ◦ Big, simple, shareable.
    ◦ Titan colour accents to tie brand subtly.
    ◦ No split screen — single strong concept image.
posts.post_caption:
What would you do with 10 free hours every week?

Most pharmacists spend that time buried in accuracy checks.
With the right system, those hours come back.

For some, it means more services.
For others, more time with patients.
For many, it simply means going home on time.

So I’ll ask again:
👉 What would you do with 10 extra hours?

#CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyOwner #PharmacyLife #PharmacyWorkflow #FutureOfPharmacy #DigitalPharmacy
posts.name: Meme – “What would you do with 10 free hours a week?” → open-ended engagement question.

posts.post_caption:
Don’t rely on manual checking.

Use TITAN AI + Barcode scanning.

Manual checking breaks the moment volume rises.
Fatigue builds. Risk increases. 😮‍💨
One interruption is all it takes for the wrong item to be picked.

Pharmacies moving to Titan see the same shift:

• Manual checking drops from doing everything to about 20–30%
• Near misses fall from several each month to a couple a year
• Workflows scale from 4,000 to 17,000 items without chaos
• AI clears most routine clinical checks before anyone touches an item
• Barcode scanning enforces the exact item-to-patient match every time

This is enforced safety.
Not memory. Not vigilance.
A system that removes the bottleneck and keeps the line moving.

👉 See how Titan transforms dispensing safety
http://bit.ly/4bEOLIK

posts.post_caption:
💥 What was the Mounjaro Bomb that hit UK pharmacy last week?

Here’s what really changed 👇

From 1 September, Mounjaro’s list price jumps by up to 170%.

But the real shock isn’t just the cost. Pharmacies must now:
1️⃣ Pay the higher price upfront
2️⃣ File monthly rebate reports (splitting NHS vs private)
3️⃣ Wait for retrospective rebates — still unclear in detail

For independents running on tight margins, that’s a cashflow crisis.
For patients, it risks confusion, delays, and unsafe online alternatives.

That’s why within days, Titan PMR launched a custom reporting suite — now used by 1,000+ pharmacies to:
✔ Generate rebate data in minutes
✔ Stay compliant without drowning in admin
✔ Keep the focus on patients, not paperwork

When global shocks land in local pharmacy…
👉 Titan moves fast.

#pharmacy #mounjaro #pmrreimagined #TitanPMR

If dispensing still eats every hour of your day…

what happens to your services, your patients, your future?

That’s the question Pritee asked herself at Howletts Pharmacy in Ruislip.

Before Titan, she was buried in baskets. Accuracy checks stacked high. End-of-month claims dragging into days. Services struggling to take off.

posts.post_caption:
Sunshine. Laughter. Basically a school sports day.

Titan BBQ 25 had it all.

Once a year, we get the whole Titan crew together for BBQ 25. A day to swap the laptops for laughter, the deadlines for a bit of good-natured rivalry, and remember what it is like to be properly outside.

🥥 Coconut shy
⚽ Football speed tests
💪 Tug of war
🏃 Sack races (with some questionable technique)
🍔 And food worth queueing twice for

It had all the magic of being back on the school field, just with fewer grazed knees and much better catering.

Thank you to every single one of you for bringing the energy, the humour and the team spirit that made BBQ 25 what it was. You turned it into more than just a day out, you made it a day to remember.

Here is to BBQ 26. Same time next year. Same team.

#TitanBBQ25 #TeamTitan #SummerCelebration

posts.idea:
“Have you seen the GP yet?”
“No, I just went to the pharmacy.”

That’s the shift. Quiet. Widespread. Already happening.

In July, NHS England released its 10-Year Plan.
Digital-first. AI-enabled. More access, less pressure on GPs.

But here’s the truth most headlines missed:

Pharmacies aren’t just helping anymore.
They’re replacing.

You’re delivering clinical care at scale.
Not as backup. As the front door.

✔ Blood pressure checks
✔ Contraception consultations
✔ Walk-in assessments for minor illness

What used to be “supporting primary care”
has become the core of it.


This isn’t just scope expansion.
It’s leadership by necessity.


And it’s time the systems, tools, and recognition caught up.


So if pharmacy is already carrying more,
why are we still treating it like a sideline?


#PrimaryCare #PharmacyFirst #PharmacyLeadership #NHSPlan #ClinicalPharmacy #DigitalHealth

Messy. Manual. Held together by goodwill and duct tape.

If pharmacy is going to lead on clinical care —
we need infrastructure that’s built for it.

That’s how we protect the people doing the work — and the patients receiving it.


It’s not about adding more trays.
It’s about removing the need for them.

posts.post_caption:
Ever clinically checked a dressing?

Sure you have. 

It’s not a controlled drug.
Not a risky interaction.
Just… a dressing.

Not because they’re dangerous. But because the system doesn’t know the difference.

Thousands of low-risk items pass through pharmacies every week.
But the system treats them all the same.
So pharmacists are forced to spend time on things that pose almost no risk —
just in case.

Smart systems like Titan filter high-risk items first.
Only the safe, low-risk prescriptions—like dressings—go to AI.
AI that’s learned from pharmacist decisions and reviews every script in seconds.

So you’re no longer bogged down by the routine.
You’re freed to focus on the clinical care that only a human can give.

It’s not about replacing pharmacists.
It’s about respecting their time.

It filters the noise.
So pharmacists can focus on what actually matters.

Imagine how different your day could feel —
if you weren’t checking dressings.

What’s one thing you wish you had more time for?

🚨 Pharmacies just ranked #1 for healthcare access.

Meanwhile?
GPs were 640% more likely to be called “hard to reach.”

New YouGov data confirms what pharmacy teams already know:
They show up — when others can’t.

Let’s break it down:

• Just 5% said pharmacies were hard to access
• For GPs? 37% — the worst of any setting
• Pharmacies ranked #1 overall — ahead of GPs, dentists, A&E and hospitals
• And when patients couldn’t get a GP appointment, most said it harmed their health

Despite rising pressure, pharmacies stayed open, trusted, and accessible.
And the public noticed:

• 77% would be concerned if their pharmacy closed
• 65% said pharmacies are personally important
• Most now want pharmacies to do even more clinical care

📌 Pharmacies aren’t a fallback. They’re the front door to the NHS.

If you believe that too — share this.

#CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyFirst #PrimaryCare #NHS #HealthcareAccess #TitanPMR #ThankYouPharmacy

posts.post_caption:
Even the best pharmacies make errors — it’s human nature.
So, how do we reduce the risk?

We train our teams to double-check.
Triple-check.
Stay alert at every step.

But constant vigilance isn’t sustainable — it leads to exhaustion, not excellence.

We often say:
“Be careful.”
“Double-check everything.”
“Don’t miss a thing.”

But here’s the real question:
Why are we still designing systems that assume people won’t make mistakes?

The safest workflows don’t rely on perfect memory or superhuman focus.
They’re built to catch errors automatically.

🧾 Every label
💊 Every item
📄 Every prescription

500 near misses prevented every single day
Not by luck — by design.

Because true safety doesn’t come from trying harder.
It comes from building better systems.

🚀 TITAN + NHS Independent Prescribing

Independent Prescribing is the future of pharmacy, and the NHS pilot is making it happen.

✅ The NHS wants all pharmacists to prescribe.
✅ 200 pharmacies are in the pilot.
✅ 40 of them use TITAN!

We’ve worked with the NHS to ensure seamless compatibility with CLEO, the prescribing system chosen for the trial.

Pharmacists can now prescribe, not just dispense—a game-changer for patient care.

📢 What’s next? The NHS will be in touch with pharmacies that signed up for the programme. Stay tuned!

posts.post_caption:
A Proud Moment for Titan PMR – EPS is Now Fully Rolled Out Across Wales

On 17th November 2023, Titan PMR made history.

We became the first PMR to deploy EPS in Wales, with Wellington Road Pharmacy in Rhyl leading the way.

Patients there were the first in Wales to experience a fully digital prescription process.

Now, EPS is fully rolled out across all Titan pharmacies in Wales, marking a major milestone in our mission to transform pharmacy care.

What does this mean for pharmacies?

✅ No more paper prescriptions → Fully digital workflow.
✅ Faster, safer dispensing → Fewer errors, better stock control.
✅ Less admin, more efficiency → Simplified reimbursements & operations.

💬 "Visiting Wellington Road Pharmacy, I witnessed firsthand the benefits of the Electronic Prescription Service, which allows GPs to send prescriptions securely online to the patient's choice of community pharmacy, eliminating the need for paper forms."
— Eluned Morgan MS, Minister for Health and Social Services

💡 This is just the beginning. At Titan PMR, we’re committed to driving the future of pharmacy innovation.

🔗 Visit https://titanpmr.com/ to see how we’re shaping what’s next.

#EPSWales #PharmacyEfficiency #DigitalPharmacy #PharmacyInnovation #BetterPatientCare

posts.idea:
SLIDE 1 — HOOK

Manual checking works.
Until scale makes it unsafe.

⸻

SLIDE 2 — THE BEHAVIOUR

Why does manual double-checking exist?

Because verification isn’t enforced at the point of action.
So people compensate.

⸻

SLIDE 3 — THE FAILURE

This works until volume breaks it.

Vigilance degrades faster than workload increases.

⸻

SLIDE 4 — THE COST

The cost lands on cognitive load.

Mental energy goes into catching errors
instead of overseeing care.

⸻

SLIDE 5 — WHAT FAILS FIRST

Dispensing stability breaks first.

When safety depends on human presence,
scale becomes a risk.

⸻

SLIDE 6 — CLOSE

Systems should absorb complexity.
People shouldn’t.
posts.post_caption:
Manual verification feels responsible.
Until it becomes the bottleneck.

People double-check because the system lets work move forward without confirmation.
That’s fine at low volume.

At scale, repetition erodes attention.
Safety becomes probabilistic.
And throughput is gated by one human checkpoint.

The cost doesn’t show up as failure straight away.
It shows up as cognitive load.

Stable dispensing isn’t about asking people to focus harder.
It’s about building systems that don’t rely on focus at all.

posts.idea:
Meme Idea (detailed execution)


• Format: Bold text-first meme.
• Visual concept:

    ◦ Large central text: “What would you do with 10 free hours a week?”
    ◦ Background: clean clock face (not cheesy stock — elegant, minimalist) or abstract time-lapse motion blur to signal “time regained.”

• Design style:

    ◦ Big, simple, shareable.
    ◦ Titan colour accents to tie brand subtly.
    ◦ No split screen — single strong concept image.
posts.post_caption:
What would you do with 10 free hours every week?

Most pharmacists spend that time buried in accuracy checks.
With the right system, those hours come back.

For some, it means more services.
For others, more time with patients.
For many, it simply means going home on time.

So I’ll ask again:
👉 What would you do with 10 extra hours?

#CommunityPharmacy #PharmacyOwner #PharmacyLife #PharmacyWorkflow #FutureOfPharmacy #DigitalPharmacy

posts.idea:
Some features you build, polish, and shout about.


Others… you quietly assume are “nice to have.”
Until pharmacists say:
“This saved me.”


We didn’t launch Patient Insights expecting fanfare.
We didn’t think nomination alerts would spark gratitude.
And we didn’t imagine automated stock adjustments would be a favourite line in feedback calls.


But here’s the funny thing:
It’s often the quietest features that create the loudest relief.


• “Now I can follow up when a patient switches pharmacies.”
• “I finally know what’s in stock — without chasing spreadsheets.”
• “I don’t lose patients without knowing.”


These little moments matter.
Not because they’re flashy — but because they remove friction, protect revenue, and bring peace of mind.


So if it’s helping pharmacists breathe easier…
maybe we should talk about it more.


What’s one “small” feature that made a big difference in your day?


#PharmacyTech #PatientInsights #PharmacySystems #DigitalHealth #PharmacyLeadership #TitanPMR

posts.idea: → Vision-setting post to align with NHS 10-Year Plan.
posts.name: Prescribe. Prevent. Protect. Pharmacy 2026.

posts.idea:
“Have you seen the GP yet?”
“No, I just went to the pharmacy.”

That’s the shift. Quiet. Widespread. Already happening.

In July, NHS England released its 10-Year Plan.
Digital-first. AI-enabled. More access, less pressure on GPs.

But here’s the truth most headlines missed:

Pharmacies aren’t just helping anymore.
They’re replacing.

You’re delivering clinical care at scale.
Not as backup. As the front door.

✔ Blood pressure checks
✔ Contraception consultations
✔ Walk-in assessments for minor illness

What used to be “supporting primary care”
has become the core of it.


This isn’t just scope expansion.
It’s leadership by necessity.


And it’s time the systems, tools, and recognition caught up.


So if pharmacy is already carrying more,
why are we still treating it like a sideline?


#PrimaryCare #PharmacyFirst #PharmacyLeadership #NHSPlan #ClinicalPharmacy #DigitalHealth
posts.name: GPs are overloaded. You’re not just helping — you’re replacing.

posts.idea:
📚 Carousel Title (Slide 1)



The Problem With Scaling on Sticky Notes
Subhead: What worked at 20 services a month… breaks at 200.


Image Suggestion:
Flat lay of scattered paper trays, sticky notes, scribbled names, NHS leaflets, and highlighters. Make it messy but familiar — instantly recognisable to any UK pharmacy professional.



😰 Slide 2 — The Chaos Slide



Headline: It starts with one sticky note.


Text:
Then five. Then a colour-coding system no one agreed on.
Suddenly you’re running a clinic with no visibility.


Image Suggestion:
Close-up of a chaotic desk with multiple overlapping notes, clipboards, and partially covered patient documents. Hand reaching for a ringing phone in the background.



🔁 Slide 3 — The Pattern Slide



Headline: Every “DIY” workflow follows the same path.


Text:
Quick fix → Makeshift system → Admin overload
You scale services, but stress scales faster.


Image Suggestion:
A domino effect visual — from one tidy tray to a cascade of overflowing paperwork. Or a humorous Kanban board taped to a cabinet door with fading Post-its.



💡 Slide 4 — The Flip Slide



Headline: What if your workflow was built to scale?


Text:
✔ Automated referrals
✔ Clinical records in one place
✔ AI safety checks in seconds


Image Suggestion:
Modern digital workspace. Clean interface on a tablet or monitor, showing a clinical services dashboard. Soft focus, warm lighting, and a calm, focused staff member.



✅ Slide 5 — The Payoff Slide



Headline: No chaos. Just calm, consistent care.


Text:
Your team isn’t chasing slips.
They’re delivering services — safely, confidently, and at scale.


Image Suggestion:
A wide shot of a well-organised consultation space. Smiling team member wrapping up a blood pressure consultation. A subtle screen in the background showing completed records or referrals.



👉 Slide 6 — CTA Slide



Headline: Tired of scaling on instinct?


Text:
There’s a better way to grow.
Let us show you the tools that make it effortless.


Image Suggestion:
Simple, bold background. Titan PMR logo.
CTA: “Discover smart service workflows at titanpmr.com”
posts.post_caption:
Sticky notes. Paper trays. Colour-coded chaos.


The official workflow of… every pharmacy trying to scale services on instinct alone.


You start with a few blood pressure checks.
Then contraception. A UTI consultation. Maybe a walk-in earache.


Suddenly, you’re running a full-blown clinic on post-its.


This is what scaling often feels like:
Messy. Manual. Held together by goodwill and duct tape.


But here’s the thing no one wants to say:


If pharmacy is going to lead on clinical care —
we need infrastructure that’s built for it.


✔ Automated booking and referrals
✔ Clinical records that live in one place
✔ AI tools that flag issues before they escalate


That’s how we protect the people doing the work — and the patients receiving it.


It’s not about adding more trays.
It’s about removing the need for them.


What’s one workflow hack you wish someone had built years ago?


#PharmacyFirst #WorkflowDesign #ClinicalPharmacy #DigitalHealth #PharmacyLeadership #NHSTech

if you weren’t checking dressings.

posts.post_caption:
Ever clinically checked a dressing?

Sure you have. 

It’s not a controlled drug.
Not a risky interaction.
Just… a dressing.

Not because they’re dangerous. But because the system doesn’t know the difference.

Thousands of low-risk items pass through pharmacies every week.
But the system treats them all the same.
So pharmacists are forced to spend time on things that pose almost no risk —
just in case.

Smart systems like Titan filter high-risk items first.
Only the safe, low-risk prescriptions—like dressings—go to AI.
AI that’s learned from pharmacist decisions and reviews every script in seconds.

So you’re no longer bogged down by the routine.
You’re freed to focus on the clinical care that only a human can give.

It’s not about replacing pharmacists.
It’s about respecting their time.

It filters the noise.
So pharmacists can focus on what actually matters.

Imagine how different your day could feel —
if you weren’t checking dressings.

What’s one thing you wish you had more time for?

#PharmacyLeadership #DigitalHealth #AutomationWithPurpose #WorkforceWellbeing #UKPharmacy #ClinicalSafety

posts.idea:
Slide 1 — Curiosity Hook (Spike Up)
Still printing referrals?
It’s 2025.
Why are we still running a paper trail for clinical care?


🖨️
Visual: Retro timestamp over a printed referral. Clunky keyboard. July 2025.


Slide 2 — Problem Reveal (Dip Down)
The real issue isn’t people.
It’s systems that can’t talk to each other.


Most PMRs still can’t:
• Access NHS records
• Send structured referrals
• Integrate with Shared Care


So you’re forced to print, retype, resend.


Visual: Frustrated pharmacist holding printout between two systems.


Slide 3 — Problem/Fear (Lowest Dip)
That’s not just annoying.
It’s unsafe.


• Manual entry = errors
• Fragmented records = missed info
• Extra admin = less time with patients


Visual: Blurred patient screen with warning icon over “No allergy data found.”


Slide 4 — Solution Hope (Rising Up)
Modern PMRs do more than dispense.
They connect.
They simplify.
They protect.


Titan reads, writes, refers — in real time.


Visual: Connected system flow. Data from GP > Pharmacy > Patient Care Loop.


Slide 5 — Validation & Proof (Peak)
Check your system. Does it offer:
✅ EPS R2 accreditation
✅ Read/write NHS access
✅ Shared Care Record integration
✅ Structured referrals
✅ Automated filtering
✅ Paperless workflow


If not — it’s holding you back.


Visual: Simple green-ticked checklist with branded “Save this” tag.


Slide 6 — CTA / Belonging (Plateau High)
Still printing referrals?
You’re not behind.
Your system is.


You deserve better.
Let your PMR do the admin —
So you can do the care.


[Insert blog/demo link]


Visual: Calm, modern screen saying: “Referral received > Assigned > Completed”
posts.post_caption:
Ever had to print something…
Just so you could type it back in?

A referral from a GP.
A clinical note.
Sometimes even just a postcode.

It’s 2025 — and some PMRs still can’t talk to the NHS properly.

We’ve moved on from dial-up at home.
But in pharmacy?
We’re still working around systems that don’t connect.

And here’s the thing.
It’s not just frustrating.
It’s risky.

Because when systems don’t share information, patients fall through the cracks.

But with the right PMR:
✅ Read/write NHS access
✅ Structured referrals in seconds
✅ No more double entry or missing data

Fewer workarounds.
More clinical headspace.
Safer, calmer care.

So no — it’s not just about efficiency.
It’s about safety, clarity, and doing your job without fighting your tools.

Still printing referrals? Maybe it’s time to upgrade.

What’s one system fix that would change your day?

#DigitalHealth #Interoperability #UKPharmacy #PrimaryCare #ClinicalSystems #PharmacyLeadership

So the question is:

What would your pharmacy do with that time back?
